words: INTERVIEWER: This conversation with Alben Halen took place at his home
in Deary on February 20, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: AH
words: I could just - in a few days, why I didn't care for cigarettes—

speaker: AH
words: I had Copenhagen, why that's good enough. But, he used Copenhagen

speaker: AH
words: and his sinuses just like I've got. He quit for over a year -

speaker: AH
words: he could see a post down there and he said, "Give me a little

speaker: AH
words: of that, and I let him take some and it wasn't more than an

speaker: AH
words: hour afterwards he said, "You got to give me another pinch of

speaker: AH
words: this Copenhagen." Then on the way coming back home in the car,

speaker: AH
words: he says, he was going up to his place and I went to mine - he

speaker: AH
words: says, "Bring me a box tomorrow morning." Well, I said, "As far

speaker: AH
words: as for that well, take the one I've got here." He said the

speaker: AH
words: habit was back just like when he quit; as soon as he took one.

speaker: SS
words: Well, that's the same way with cigarettes.

speaker: AH
words: I suppose,,

speaker: SS
words: I know people that stopped for years; one cigarette does it all

speaker: SS
words: over again.

speaker: AH
words: Well, alcohol I guess is about the same way. You take a real

speaker: AH
words: alcoholic, if they take one drink they know why - they're cured,

speaker: AH
words: well one drink will start them off again. And I know two or three

speaker: AH
words: guys-that way - they quit. Just one little swallow and it will

speaker: AH
words: put them right back.

speaker: SS
words: Were lumberjacks as hard drinkers as they say? Did they drink

speaker: SS
words: a lot?

speaker: AH
words: Well, there was bell drinkers in those -they'd get thdir paychecks -

speaker: AH
words: why, then there was drinking, then after that, why sobered up and

speaker: AH
words: went back on the job. There wasn't much drinking. I never knew

speaker: AH
words: of anybody when Iworked in camp, 'cept, that carried any liquor

speaker: AH
words: in. But, most of them used Copenhagen or some cigarettes, of

speaker: AH
words: course. If it was a dry summer, you wasn't allowed to smoke in

speaker: AH
words: the woods. You could go and sit down on alog and smoke acigarette

speaker: AH
words: if you was sure it wasn't hanging in your mouth when you was sawing

speaker: AH
words: or swamping or anything. Scared of fire in those days.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the fire that they had here -the one in 1923 that

speaker: SS
words: burned down a town?

speaker: AH
words: Oh, yeah, I remember that.

speaker: SS
words: How did it start - what do...

speaker: AH
words: I wasn't working in camp at that time but Iremember the fire all

speaker: AH
words: right. My brother, Oscar, was working up there. Started just

speaker: AH
words: a little ways off from the camp there in the driest part of the

speaker: AH
words: summer. I didn't work there, but at that time I was out in the

speaker: AH
words: farm in the summer. It was that harvest anyway. I remember when

speaker: AH
words: they had the big fire and Idon't know, in Coeur d'Alene and all

speaker: AH
words: the way down. That was 1919, and the air up in here was - you

speaker: AH
words: could stand and look at the sun and then it wouldn't hurt your

speaker: AH
words: eyes for .days from the smoke. Iguess that's the worst part we

speaker: AH
words: had at the time. I don't think they had any fires when I was

speaker: AH
words: right around close here, though. They had the fires afew times

speaker: AH
words: started up above. Up in that country, of course, but in here,

speaker: AH
words: why Idon't think they ever -oh, they had one down in Kendrick,

speaker: AH
words: that burnt there one time. I don't remember what year that was.

speaker: AH
words: Burnt up ahaystack and burnt along the side in there. I could

speaker: AH
words: see the smoke but I never went down and looked at it.

speaker: SS
words: What about the time that Deary burned down - that the town here

speaker: SS
words: did? Were you in town at all to fight on the fire?

speaker: AH
words: When Deary burned?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: AH
words: Well, Deary burnt two or three different times.

speaker: SS
words: It did?

speaker: AH
words: Oh yeah. The first building - I stood right there where that Conners

speaker: AH
words: Cafe is at, and that thing - well, that wasn't the first one.

speaker: AH
words: There used to be a butcher shop and the whole where that store

speaker: AH
words: is at along in there. A big shed outside and the store and a meat

speaker: AH
words: market and that thing caught on fire. One evening I was up here,

speaker: AH
words: folks lived up in this end of town. Old Gartland did - and I went

speaker: AH
words: over and visited with him across the street before I went home,

speaker: AH
words: and he looked out the window and he said the whole town is burning*

speaker: AH
words: We ran down and I went up and walked in - he lived in the other

speaker: AH
words: building up on the corner out there and pounded on the door and

speaker: AH
words: said, "Your store is one fire." It wasn't more than a few minutes

speaker: AH
words: before it burned down. So, right where that Odd Fellows Hall is

speaker: AH
words: at, there's some building behind, owned by old lady, mother to

speaker: AH
words: Frank Jean. My brother was working for Jean there at that time,

speaker: AH
words: Robert was. I went up and walked in across Franklin and Second

speaker: AH
words: at the hospital and I went down there and asked her if she was

speaker: AH
words: scared. "No," she said* "I'm not scared, but if anything happens,"

speaker: AH
words: she said, "I've got my coat on and I'll get out." But it's right

speaker: AH
words: across the street from where that other building burned. Then

speaker: AH
words: another store - it was a drug store first, and the store and then

speaker: AH
words: they changed that over to a beer parlor - that thing burned

speaker: AH
words: down during the night. I don't know - I wasn't here at that time.

speaker: AH
words: That went down. But they all burned separately just about.

speaker: AH
words: Jorgenson has a store where the garage is at - the big wooden

speaker: AH
words: building.. That went up in smoke, but that was after the other

speaker: AH
words: part of town burned. They cleaned out there where that brick

speaker: AH
words: building was at - that was a big hall up on the upstairs and

speaker: AH
words: a fellow by the name of Peterson had that.. That burned during

speaker: AH
words: the night. I didn't know about it until the next morning when

speaker: AH
words: we drove into town, but I think three or four buildings burned

speaker: AH
words: there. Then that hotel - that stood right out from the street

speaker: AH
words: across from Lawrence's store on the other side. That beer

speaker: AH
words: parlor - that burned up, that wasn't too long ago either. So

speaker: AH
words: the oldest building that is left is the one where the Odd Fellows

speaker: AH
words: Hall is at - that was a saloon building before. Before they

speaker: AH
words: fixed that over to a grocery store, Frankie had in there. I

speaker: AH
words: guess that's about the last of that hall.

speaker: SS
words: Which is the fire that burned a lot of the buildings, except

speaker: SS
words: they saved the bank?

speaker: AH
words: Well, that was when that hall burnt. There was a store, and there

speaker: AH
words: was a building up above that, that was a beer parlor, or a pool

speaker: AH
words: hall. That burned and there was a building or two this way- I

speaker: AH
words: don't remember who lived in them, that burned* I believe the

speaker: AH
words: the one that burned, probably was the post office that burnt.

speaker: AH
words: Or Devoigns had a restaurant, I think - that burned down. But

speaker: AH
words: I always had it in my head that was where they had the post office,

speaker: AH
words: No, there was quite a few buildings that went that time.

speaker: SS
words: Well what was the deal -they didn't have any water?

speaker: AH
words: No, they didn't have no water. Unless they hauled it in the tank

speaker: AH
words: or drilled water some way -I don't think they even had adrilled

speaker: AH
words: well. For many years there they didn't -to start with. Right

speaker: AH
words: across from where that hotel is sitting,ror that, what they call

speaker: AH
words: it the park where they got that shed. Right down in the draw

speaker: AH
words: there was astrong spring -they took ahold and took water from

speaker: AH
words: that spring to start with. But Iwas thinking, there was another

speaker: AH
words: building that burnt after that. Iguess he had jewelry store

speaker: AH
words: there that went up in smoke at that time too. It was out pretty

speaker: AH
words: well and them brick buildings, they was put up afterwards. One

speaker: AH
words: building was moved. Was moved off from the corner there and moved

speaker: AH
words: up above above the bank on that corner and that Reliefson that,

speaker: AH
words: that burned, but Ithink that's the only building that burned at

speaker: AH
words: that time.

speaker: SS
words: So did most of the other buildings there besides the bank, most

speaker: SS
words: of those other buildings burned right around it then?

speaker: AH
words: Yeah. Right around this way. Down as far as, Ithink, where the -

speaker: AH
words: the post office was down in there, down there too, you know, I

speaker: AH
words: think that burned out at that time too, and everything that was

speaker: AH
words: in there.

speaker: SS
words: Did they just rebuild the town right away? Did it take very long

speaker: SS
words: to put them buildings back in?

speaker: AH
words: Well, they put in where Lerder's store is at, that's two different

speaker: AH
words: buildings, you know. They cut the hole and connected them up,

speaker: AH
words: see, otherwise, that belonged to some other party. I don't know

speaker: AH
words: exactly who has it. Up there were Lawrence's - Jerry Lawrence

speaker: AH
words: is at, that was a building, I guess, in there too that burnt.

speaker: AH
words: I think there where the post office is at. No, I figured when

speaker: AH
words: I woke up in the morning, why we drove uptown and we'd been

speaker: AH
words: there the evening before and bought a bunch of groceries in that

speaker: AH
words: store that burnt up. That was nothing but ashes there. I said

speaker: AH
words: to my brother, "We're just hauling the grain to the warehouse in time

speaker: AH
words: to get the whole thing burnt." We had about fifteen hundred sacks

speaker: AH
words: of grain in there. But that didn't burn. I guess, if it would

speaker: AH
words: have, there would've been no insurance on it or nothing at that

speaker: AH
words: time. But the later years they changed that so as soon as you

speaker: AH
words: owned anything you had insurance on it* I didn't carry any insur

speaker: AH
words: ance on the farm when the grain's out0 I could've had that trans

speaker: AH
words: ferred up here but I didn't even have any insurance on the grain

speaker: AH
words: there so I didn't bother with it* Everything was all right.

speaker: SS
words: So the warehouse didn't burn at all?

speaker: AH
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: And lucky...

speaker: AH
words: I was lucky at that. No, that is really the oldest building of

speaker: AH
words: the first buildings. That's the third building that was built

speaker: AH
words: in Deary.

speaker: SS
words: Which one?

speaker: AH
words: Where they got the Odd Fellows Hall where that saloon is at - that's

speaker: AH
words: still standing there. Then they had -. on that, below on this side

speaker: AH
words: coming down the highway, they had a dance hall and the show - run

speaker: AH
words: the show there - that darned thing burned up.

speaker: SS
words: Where was that at?

speaker: AH
words: That's coming this way down from the - on that side of the highway

speaker: AH
words: coming down aways. I don't know exactly where it stood, but

speaker: AH
words: it must be down in there someplace. Like some of them older

speaker: AH
words: houses  like the living houses, they're still standing. A lot of them are still here.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know who homesteaded here before the town started up?

speaker: AH
words: Well, Iheard many arguments, Iwouldn't say which one, but they

speaker: AH
words: said Blalock had one part, but Iwon't say that's farther down,

speaker: AH
words: they used to say I was always thinking that Stockwell had one

speaker: AH
words: and Blalock had the other one on this end of it, I don't know.

speaker: AH
words: I wouldn't say that for sure though. It didn't look anything

speaker: AH
words: like now, you know* in there at that time. Like the house that

speaker: AH
words: the folks had up there and the house that Reed's gotten in

speaker: AH
words: them houses - they went in there pretty soon. Then all the

speaker: AH
words: old farmers out there, they went in and build a living house,

speaker: AH
words: Barkland, John Carson and Applquist.and Nels Burkland ,and they

speaker: AH
words: all had buildings up along - and I think they are all standing.

speaker: AH
words: Up along the street -there, going up, Idon't know who owns the

speaker: AH
words: place now, but built the house there. His daughter,

speaker: AH
words: Jessie Andreasen oh, what the heck, what's her name--A was the tirst

speaker: AH
words: kid born in this town, so they gave herlot. He built the house

speaker: AH
words: and I think that's still standing there.

speaker: SS
words: Got the lot for free?

speaker: AH
words: He gave her a free lot because she was the first..

speaker: AH
words: He built the store down there by Reed's, to make it in a

speaker: AH
words: hurry, you see. He didn't know the town wasn't even big - he

speaker: AH
words: bought that store that burned up there where the garage is at.

speaker: AH
words: That was store. He had a place down at Nora before

speaker: AH
words: and when they started the town, why, he thought they was going to

speaker: AH
words: start it down there, see. So he bought up a building, but that

speaker: AH
words: store down here and he built up here. That house that Lawrence's

speaker: AH
words: got up there, a big house - that Jorgensem built that house -

speaker: AH
words: that1was his home. I guess he sold out to - I think he sold out

speaker: AH
words: to Rolle

speaker: SS
words: Was Jorgensn's store a very good one?

speaker: AH
words: It's a big - covered quite a ground and then Bjoro had it and

speaker: AH
words: it - when it burned up it belonged to Bjora

speaker: SS
words: I heard Jorgenson used to undersell the other stores.

speaker: AH
words: Yeah, he did. He never lived— he lived at Nora before he come

speaker: AH
words: here, see. All that was there was a saloon and a store. He

speaker: AH
words: had a store and I guess - I'm not sure, unless there was another

speaker: AH
words: store in there, but I don't think they even had a post office.

speaker: AH
words: I don't know where that building burned up there across the creek

speaker: AH
words: over here, where you go out of town aways - about two miles - where

speaker: AH
words: that road forks and goes that way and this little house that burned

speaker: AH
words: up and they put in a trailer house* Well, you see that's where

speaker: AH
words: father to Mike; Cluver lived - he had a store there and he had

speaker: AH
words: the post office. That was Anderson's post office. Anderson's

speaker: AH
words: post office started the first post office they ever had, I guess.

speaker: AH
words: This wasn't where I lived, over where they had the Kleths living.

speaker: AH
words: That's where he homesteaded and he got the mail there once a week,

speaker: AH
words: or twice and Dean Anderson. This was Anderson's post office when

speaker: AH
words: Cluver had it - it never changed names - it's Anderson's post

speaker: AH
words: office. So when Deary was built, why, they changed the name to

speaker: AH
words: Deary and Anderson's Post Office was taken away and von went

speaker: AH
words: down there* Had a post office there, but you know, that was Vasgar

speaker: AH
words: before. It wasn't Avon when it was Vassar* No, it made quite

speaker: AH
words: a little change in things.

speaker: SS
words: Did Avon lose its post office too when Deary came in?

speaker: AH
words: They didn't for awhile, but after awhile they put it up here.

speaker: AH
words: Take Avon on the route now.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when you say it made quite a little change, what do you

speaker: SS
words: mean? Did all the people move into Deary?

speaker: AH
words: Well, all the change was made when they changed the name, you

speaker: AH
words: know, lots of sent by mistake to Anderson's post office and it

speaker: AH
words: was Deary. Had to get that straightened up. A lot of people

speaker: AH
words: never knew that there was anything like that, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did a whole bunch of people move into Deary real quick

speaker: SS
words: when the town started up - a lot of new people come in?

speaker: AH
words: Yes, quite a few got in here* But then, farmers they bought them

speaker: AH
words: buildings, and built them houses - hardly ever lived there* They

speaker: AH
words: rented them out all the time. But, you know, there was a bunch

speaker: AH
words: of carpenters come in here - I think their name was Grangers - I

speaker: AH
words: wouldn't swear to that, but they built an awful lot of houses.

speaker: AH
words: I knew - shouldn't say this about the man, anyway, but - fellow

speaker: AH
words: was married to my sister, his name was Swenson. They was building

speaker: AH
words: that hotel and he, Louie was painting and you know, to make all

speaker: AH
words: them rooms it's a lot of work* One morning he asked me if I would

speaker: AH
words: take- the old horse down and pack up a sack of spuds he'd bought.

speaker: AH
words: So I took the old saddle on the horse to come down and he was

speaker: AH
words: laughing so big and I was asking him what he was laughing at. He

speaker: AH
words: dumped them spuds into sacks and tied them on.the saddle. They

speaker: AH
words: had this old Orrie and was washing his whiskers and his hair -

speaker: AH
words: he went and stuck his head in the paint bucket - drunk. (Laughter)

speaker: AH
words: He had long whiskers - way down - and I don't know if he got it

speaker: AH
words: in his eyes or not, but his whiskers fell in the paint bucket.

speaker: AH
words: They .had him out there washing paint out of...

speaker: SS
words: What did he do with the spuds?

speaker: AH
words: Well, I took them up to spud hill there, to my sister's place.

speaker: AH
words: I was He was married to my sister and staying up there. A kid you

speaker: AH
words: know, he tied that sack on the saddle horse and Iwalked up and

speaker: AH
words: tied the horse up and got up there, I don't know, I thought I

speaker: AH
words: had enough to handle the sack or not. My sister came out there

speaker: AH
words: and we took them off the horse all right. I got the saddle

speaker: AH
words: off the horse too, as far as that goes No, lots of funny things

speaker: AH
words: happened there. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he caught it from her pretty bad? From your sister?

speaker: AH
words: No, the more he got drunk - that fellow he was living there in

speaker: AH
words: the house. He went and got drunk and my brother was painting

speaker: AH
words: and he had a paint bucket standing there, my brother-in-law, and

speaker: AH
words: he went and stuck his whiskers in there and fell down. Oh,

speaker: AH
words: I remember them guys in there pretty well, we as far as

speaker: AH
words: that goes* Fellow by the name of Sale.ne and Albert Pearson, they

speaker: AH
words: was the one that built that building there where the saloon was

speaker: AH
words: at. They was such a hurry, that Troy guy came up and put in the

speaker: AH
words: bar in the front end of the hotel while they was building the

speaker: AH
words: saloon, so it wasn't short the liquor or anything. No, I...

speaker: SS
words: Salene - he came from Troy didn't he?

speaker: AH
words: Salane?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: AH
words: Yeah. And Albert Pearson. They had the saloon down there, you

speaker: AH
words: see and moved it up there and I guess they added on to it probably,

speaker: AH
words: I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Didn't Swan Ericson move up here from Troy? I think he'd been- down

speaker: SS
words: there.

speaker: AH
words: Swan Ericson moved off of the place I guess. Yeah, he probably

speaker: AH
words: lived in Troy for awhile. He was a blacksmith here - he had a

speaker: AH
words: place out here south on -well, you know where that Dry Creek cemetery

speaker: AH
words: is, well his place is just on the east side of that road and the

speaker: AH
words: cemetery on the west, I think* I think four of them fellows owned

speaker: AH
words: places in there and donated land for a cemetery*.

speaker: SS
words: Right where they met at the corner?

speaker: AH
words: In the corner, yeah* No, they got that tangled up too, that ceme

speaker: AH
words: tery stuff There was two people buried there and didn't know -

speaker: AH
words: a ain't never knew who they was. Buried at night. One -theyAmarked itone

speaker: AH
words: was right in the drive-way where they drive over it. But they

speaker: AH
words: tried to trace who it was and somebody said they thought it's a

speaker: AH
words: Holiday - they wasn't sure. But there's two graves there that

speaker: AH
words: nobody knew who they was. Funny things have happened, you know,

speaker: AH
words: everything was wild and they had aman looking at the cemetery and

speaker: AH
words: that wasnt more than just try to get the lots in it. But

speaker: AH
words: you know they came pretty near to missin' some of the graves there

speaker: AH
words: when they surveyed it. But it wasn't worse and they let it

speaker: AH
words: pass. I dug lots of graves in there, as far as that goes.

speaker: SS
words: Is that hard soil to dig through?

speaker: AH
words: Oh, it's harder than heck down in the bottom. Clay. I dug one

speaker: AH
words: grave there for akid alone. It was for akid so Ididn't dig

speaker: AH
words: more than that one four feet. There were graves down there we had to get

speaker: AH
words: them down to six feet - and you know they've changed all that

speaker: AH
words: stuff. They never dig a grave that deep any more. Dig them

speaker: AH
words: about five feet and when they get the cement blocks around, get

speaker: AH
words: the coffin in and I don't think they're more than about that far

speaker: AH
words: from top down to the... Somebody dug agrave up there, six feet

speaker: AH
words: deep* Going to bury the guy, why they didn't have them straps

speaker: AH
words: that they got now when they let them down; they wasn't long enough.

speaker: AH
words: They had to get abunch of ropes and stuff to put it down. Now

speaker: AH
words: they tried to get by as easiest as they can I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when do you start hitting the clay pretty bad? Right close

speaker: SS
words: to the surface.

speaker: AH
words: Well, for the first two feet you can go down pretty good and after

speaker: AH
words: that, why, it got harder and harder. No, Iwas with lots of that

speaker: AH
words: they grave digging, you know. Somebody died, "somebody to dig.

speaker: AH
words: Probably five or six men would go dig that grave. There was no

speaker: AH
words: charging or anything like that. Nobody ever charged for it. Now

speaker: AH
words: adays, why it's different. You get one of them back-hoes and dig

speaker: AH
words: it out. I guess you pay off from $25 to $50 for a grave.

speaker: SS
words: Did it take a very long time to dig in that clay?

speaker: AH
words: No, it didn't take too awful long* You know, you can't work more

speaker: AH
words: than two men in a grave - two men can work in there, of course,

speaker: AH
words: one on each end. You'd work like heck when you're down in there,

speaker: AH
words: but you don't stay there too long till you tire out and somebody

speaker: AH
words: else would take it. Over on this other cemet ry there we had

speaker: AH
words: one awful time. A guy died - Arwick died and we was going to dig

speaker: AH
words: a grave, so we got up and started digging and all at once, why,

speaker: AH
words: they got in the head they was going to shoot it "Shoot it??

speaker: AH
words: No, let's not shoot it." "Oh, yes, we'll shoot it." It's much

speaker: AH
words: easier than digging. So they put in a charge of dynamite and dug

speaker: AH
words: down, loosened up the dirt, and when they got that done why,

speaker: AH
words: now the grave was made a year or two right beside it...everything

speaker: AH
words: was water and that side from the other grave and into what they

speaker: AH
words: was digging* But that happened one time* They had planks and

speaker: AH
words: everything else in there, I don't know if they ever got it out.

speaker: AH
words: Water and you know, what it smells like coming from another grave.

speaker: AH
words: No, the way they dig them nowadays, why they can dig in harder

speaker: AH
words: ground. Well, it wasn't harder than we could dig it.

speaker: SS
words: Well, which cemetery was that? That wasn't the Dry Creek?

speaker: AH
words: No, that wasn't Dry Creek. It's up here where this church is

speaker: AH
words: standing, right out of town on the south side where he was buried.

speaker: AH
words: No, the biggest grave that I ever helped to dig was room enough

speaker: AH
words: to set three coffins down at the same time.

speaker: SS
words: Why was that?

speaker: AH
words: Well, got in a car wreck and killed Joe Whybark and Mrs. Whybark

speaker: AH
